question: Which process type is best characterized by highly customized, low-volume production where each job differs significantly from the next?
options: Assembly line — products move through a fixed sequence of workstations at a controlled pace
Continuous flow — materials move through the system without interruption around the clock
Job shop — work is organized around flexible equipment capable of handling diverse, custom orders
Batch process — identical items are grouped and produced together in discrete runs
explanation: A job shop handles highly customized, low-volume work where each order may require a different routing through the facility. Skilled workers and general-purpose equipment are used to accommodate variety. Assembly lines are suited to high-volume standardized products. Continuous flow (e.g., oil refining) operates 24/7 on a single product. Batch processes produce moderate volumes of similar items together but do not offer the extreme customization of a job shop.

question: Which facility layout would a hospital most typically use for its clinical departments such as radiology, surgery, and the emergency room?
options: Product layout — departments arranged sequentially to match the steps of a single standardized treatment
Fixed-position layout — patients remain in one location and all staff and equipment come to them
Cellular layout — small self-contained units each handle a complete family of related procedures
Process layout — similar functions and equipment are grouped together in dedicated departments
explanation: Hospitals use a process layout: radiology, surgery, emergency, and other departments are grouped by function. Patients (the 'jobs') move between departments as needed depending on their individual care requirements. A product layout would require patients to follow a fixed, identical sequence — inappropriate for the variety of medical needs. Fixed-position layout applies when the object being worked on cannot move (like a ship or a building). Cellular layout is more common in manufacturing than in hospital settings.

question: Toyota's Georgetown plant produces Camry sedans on a moving assembly line, with each car following the same sequence of workstations. Which process type does this represent?
options: Job shop, because Toyota customizes each vehicle to the buyer's specifications before building it
Batch process, because cars are grouped by color and trim level before entering the line
Assembly line, because a standardized product moves through a fixed sequence at a controlled pace
Continuous flow, because the plant operates without stopping for 24 hours per day
explanation: An assembly line is characterized by a standardized product moving through a fixed sequence of workstations at a controlled, paced rate. Toyota's Camry plant is the textbook example. A job shop would produce highly variable, custom orders. Batch processing involves discrete runs of similar items, not a continuously moving line. Continuous flow (like oil refining or paper manufacturing) runs non-stop on a single undifferentiated product without discrete units.

question: Which statement correctly defines a continuous flow process?
options: A process where materials flow without interruption through dedicated equipment, typically producing a single undifferentiated product
A process where workers continuously improve quality by pausing the line whenever a defect is detected
A process where customer orders flow continuously into a job shop and are assigned to workers in real time
A process where a product moves continuously from one batch run to the next without changeover delays
explanation: Continuous flow processes (also called process industries) run materials through specialized, dedicated equipment without stopping — examples include oil refining, chemical manufacturing, and paper mills. The output is typically a single homogeneous product or commodity. Option A describes andon cord / jidoka practices from lean manufacturing. Option C describes order management, not a process type. Option D conflates continuous flow with batch processing.

question: On the product-process matrix, a firm that produces moderate volumes of a limited variety of products most likely occupies which position?
options: Upper-left corner — high variety, low volume, job shop process
Lower-right corner — single standardized product, continuous flow process
Middle of the matrix — moderate variety and volume, batch or assembly line process
Upper-right corner — high variety, high volume, continuous flow process
explanation: The product-process matrix maps product variety and volume to the appropriate process type. Moderate variety at moderate volume falls in the middle of the matrix, where batch processes or disconnected assembly lines are most appropriate. The upper-left corner houses job shops (high variety, low volume). The lower-right corner houses continuous flow (low variety, very high volume). The upper-right corner is typically off-diagonal — high variety at high volume is rarely sustainable because it implies both customization and scale simultaneously.

question: A manufacturing plant is described as follows: machines are grouped into self-contained teams, each team handles a complete family of similar parts from start to finish, and workers in each team are cross-trained to operate multiple machines. Which layout does this describe?
options: Process layout — all lathes are grouped together, all milling machines are grouped together
Fixed-position layout — the product stays in one place and workers rotate to it
Cellular layout — machines are grouped into cells that handle a complete product family
Product layout — machines are arranged in the sequence required to produce a single product
explanation: Cellular layout (or cellular manufacturing) groups dissimilar machines into self-contained cells, each dedicated to a family of similar parts. Workers are cross-trained to handle all operations within the cell, reducing material handling and setup time. Process layout groups machines by type (all lathes together), not by product family. Fixed-position layout keeps the product stationary. Product layout arranges machines in the exact sequence for one specific product, not a family.

question: An aerospace company is assembling a commercial aircraft. Workers, tools, and sub-assemblies are brought to the aircraft as it sits in a fixed location in the hangar. Which layout is this, and why is it appropriate?
options: Process layout — because aircraft assembly requires many specialized functional departments
Cellular layout — because each aircraft model is treated as a separate product family requiring its own cell
Product layout — because the sequence of assembly steps is fixed and identical for every aircraft
Fixed-position layout — because the product is too large and heavy to move through a facility
explanation: Fixed-position layout is used when the product is too large, heavy, or fragile to move through a facility. Aircraft, ships, large construction projects, and bridges are classic examples. All resources (workers, components, tools) come to the stationary product. Process layout groups functions but doesn't keep the product fixed. Cellular layout would be used for smaller parts or sub-assemblies feeding into the main aircraft, not the final assembly. Product layout moves the product along a line, which is impractical for a 60-ton aircraft.

question: A custom furniture maker operates as a job shop. As a new online retail channel doubles its order volume, management considers converting to a batch process. Which tradeoff must management accept if they make this change?
options: Batch processing will reduce the firm's ability to accept highly customized individual orders in exchange for higher efficiency on standard designs
Batch processing will increase unit costs because it requires more specialized equipment than job shops
Batch processing requires hiring more workers per unit of output compared to the job shop model
Batch processing increases material waste because products cannot be inspected between process steps
explanation: Converting from job shop to batch process improves efficiency on standard product lines but reduces flexibility. Batch processes are designed for runs of similar items — they cannot economically accommodate the highly varied, one-off orders that define a job shop. Management must decide whether the growing standard-item volume justifies sacrificing custom-order capability. Option A is incorrect — batch processes typically lower unit costs through economies of scale. Option C is backwards; batch processes generally reduce labor per unit. Option D mischaracterizes batch processing quality control.

question: A plant producing standard automotive parts currently uses a process layout. A lean consultant recommends converting to cellular layout. Which specific operational improvement is the primary justification for this recommendation?
options: Cellular layout reduces material travel distance and work-in-process inventory by grouping machines that serve a common part family near each other
Cellular layout allows the plant to produce a wider variety of custom parts because cells are flexible
Cellular layout maximizes equipment utilization by ensuring every machine runs at full capacity during every shift
Cellular layout eliminates the need for scheduling because workers self-assign jobs within their cell
explanation: The primary lean benefit of cellular layout over process layout is reduced material travel and work-in-process inventory. In a process layout, parts travel long distances between departments (all lathes, then all mills, then all grinders) and accumulate in queues between steps. Cells bring the relevant machines together so parts move short distances and queue times collapse. Option A overstates flexibility — cells are dedicated to a part family, not unlimited variety. Option C is wrong — cellular layout does not maximize per-machine utilization; it trades utilization for flow. Option D is an exaggeration.

question: A pharmaceutical company produces aspirin in a continuous flow process. A strategic review suggests expanding into personalized medicine requiring dozens of unique formulations per batch. Why would this expansion almost certainly require a fundamental process type change?
options: Continuous flow is optimized for a single undifferentiated product at very high volume; producing dozens of formulations requires either batch or job-shop processes that allow changeovers between runs
Continuous flow processes cannot be operated safely with multiple chemical formulations due to contamination risk alone
Regulatory agencies prohibit pharmaceutical companies from using continuous flow processes for any prescription drugs
Personalized medicine requires fixed-position layouts because each patient's medication must be assembled in a dedicated location
explanation: Continuous flow processes are designed around a single homogeneous product running through dedicated, specialized equipment without stopping. Switching to dozens of unique formulations requires frequent changeovers, different process parameters, and varied recipes — capabilities that continuous flow equipment lacks. Batch processes or job-shop configurations provide the flexibility for variety. Contamination risk (option A) is a real concern but is a secondary operational issue, not the fundamental structural reason. Option C is false. Fixed-position layout (option D) is designed for large, immovable products — not individual drug formulations.

question: According to the product-process matrix, what strategic risk does a firm face when its product-process position drifts significantly off the diagonal?
options: Off-diagonal firms face higher labor costs because they cannot optimize staffing for their process type

Off-diagonal positioning creates misalignment between operational capabilities and market requirements, making the firm either overbuilt for the volume it serves or too rigid for the variety customers demand
Off-diagonal firms are prohibited from using lean manufacturing tools because their processes are incompatible
Moving off the diagonal is always a temporary condition that self-corrects as markets mature
explanation: The diagonal of the product-process matrix represents alignment between product variety/volume and process type. Firms above the diagonal have more flexibility (process capability) than their volume justifies — they incur unnecessary costs. Firms below the diagonal have more rigid processes than their variety requires — they cannot serve customer needs. Both misalignments create competitive vulnerability. Option A is a possible symptom, not the root strategic risk. Option C has no basis in operations theory. Option D is false; off-diagonal positioning can persist indefinitely if uncorrected.

question: A company begins as a custom machine shop (job shop) and grows over 10 years into a firm producing a few standard product lines in high volume. Which sequence of process type transitions does the product-process matrix predict this firm should follow?
options: Job shop → continuous flow → batch process → assembly line
Job shop → batch process → assembly line → continuous flow (if the product becomes a commodity)
Job shop → fixed-position → cellular layout → product layout
Job shop → assembly line → batch process → continuous flow
explanation: The product-process matrix shows that as product volume increases and variety decreases, firms should migrate along the diagonal: from job shop to batch, then to assembly line, and potentially to continuous flow if the product becomes fully standardized and commoditized. Option A skips batch, jumping illogically to continuous flow first. Option C conflates layout types with process types. Option D reverses the order — assembly lines precede batch in Option D, but batch processes come before assembly lines on the matrix as volume grows.

question: A Boeing 787 Dreamliner is assembled using a fixed-position layout in Everett, Washington. Critics argue Boeing should switch to a moving assembly line to increase production rate. Which operational argument best supports maintaining the fixed-position layout for this product?
options: Fixed-position layout is always cheaper than assembly lines because it requires fewer material handling systems
The 787's size, weight, complexity, and low production volume make moving it through stations impractical; the cost and risk of line crashes outweigh throughput gains
Boeing has a government contract requirement to use fixed-position layout for all commercial aircraft
Fixed-position layout maximizes quality because workers can specialize on a single aircraft for its entire build cycle
explanation: Fixed-position layout is appropriate for large, complex, low-volume products like commercial aircraft. Moving an 87-meter-long, partially-assembled aircraft between stations introduces significant logistical complexity, risk of damage, and coordination challenges that may outweigh throughput gains. Boeing has experimented with moving lines but at much slower speeds than automotive assembly lines. Option A is false — fixed-position layouts often require more material handling, not less. Option C has no basis. Option D is partially true but is not the primary argument; quality can be managed in both layouts.

question: A consumer electronics firm produces ten product families, each requiring different components and assembly steps. Management is choosing between a process layout (group all soldering stations, then all testing stations) and a cellular layout (create ten cells, each handling one product family end-to-end). Which scenario would most favor the process layout over cellular?
options: Demand for each product family is stable and predictable, with each family requiring roughly equal production volume
Equipment utilization rates are already very high and adding dedicated machines for each cell would require significant capital investment
Workers are already cross-trained and comfortable moving between different product families during a single shift
Lead time reduction is the firm's primary competitive priority and customers are willing to pay for faster delivery
explanation: Cellular layout dedicates equipment to each product family — which means each cell needs its own machines even if those machines sit idle part of the time. When equipment is already highly utilized and purchasing additional machines is costly, sharing expensive equipment across product families (process layout) is financially superior. Options A and C actually favor cellular layout — stable demand and cross-trained workers make cell operation more effective. Option D also favors cellular layout because cells dramatically reduce lead times by eliminating inter-department queuing.

question: Dell's original build-to-order model required a different process configuration than a traditional PC manufacturer selling pre-built computers through retail. Which process type combination best characterizes Dell's original model versus the traditional model?
options: Dell used continuous flow; traditional manufacturers used job shops to produce standardized retail products
Dell used a job shop for final assembly customization; traditional manufacturers used batch processes to build retail inventory
Dell used a batch-to-order hybrid process; traditional manufacturers used fixed-position layouts for retail display units
Dell used an assembly line triggered by individual customer orders; traditional manufacturers used assembly lines running to finished-goods inventory forecasts
explanation: Both Dell and traditional PC manufacturers used assembly lines — the process type was similar. The key difference was the trigger and inventory strategy: Dell's line ran only when an order arrived (make-to-order), keeping no finished-goods inventory. Traditional manufacturers ran their lines to forecast (make-to-stock), producing inventory speculatively and pushing it into retail channels. This distinction shows that process type and inventory strategy are separate OM decisions that together define the overall system. Options A and B incorrectly assign process types. Option C introduces fixed-position layout, which is irrelevant to PC manufacturing.
